results.title: SecondSwap_Marketplace vesting listing order affects how much the vesting buyers can claim at a given step
results.reasoning: Yes. Audit finding #11 (“VestingManager stepsClaimed Contamination Corrupts Buyer Vesting Schedules”) is the same issue as the described vulnerability. Both explain that when the first listing for a vesting plan is transferred to VestingManager, its stepsClaimed is inherited and then incorrectly reused for subsequent listings/buyers, causing buyer claimable amounts to depend on listing order. They reference the same code path (SecondSwap_StepVesting._createVesting handling of stepsClaimed for the VestingManager beneficiary and the marketplace/manager listing-transfer flow). Fixing the contamination of stepsClaimed (e.g., correctly tracking/update stepsClaimed per transfer or isolating per-listing state) would resolve both. Thus, the audit detected the exact vulnerability.

results.title: transferVesting creates an incorrect vesting for new users when they purchase a vesting, because stepsClaimed is the same for all sales, allowing an attacker to prematurely unlock too many tokens
results.reasoning: Yes. Audit item 11 “VestingManager stepsClaimed Contamination Corrupts Buyer Vesting Schedules” describes the same flaw: the VestingManager aggregates vestings under one beneficiary entry in StepVesting, retaining a single (stale) stepsClaimed value from the first seller. Subsequent buyers inherit this incorrect stepsClaimed, enabling premature claims. It references the same code path (VestingManager listing/transfer to StepVesting, StepVesting._createVesting for existing beneficiary not updating stepsClaimed) and impact (early token unlocks/incorrect schedules). A fix that correctly tracks/transfers per-listing stepsClaimed (or updates stepsClaimed on transfer) would resolve both, so the vulnerability is the same.

results.title: In transferVesting, the grantorVesting.releaseRate is calculated incorrectly, which leads to the sender being able to unlock more tokens than were initially locked.
results.reasoning: Yes. The audit report’s Issue 10 explicitly identifies the same bug: in SecondSwap_StepVesting.transferVesting, grantorVesting.releaseRate is set to totalAmount/numOfSteps instead of using remaining amounts and remaining steps. It explains the same overclaim/stealing scenario, references the same code path, and implies the same fix of basing releaseRate on (totalAmount - amountClaimed)/(numOfSteps - stepsClaimed). Fixing the report’s issue would fix the described vulnerability.
